name: Oisin
description: Oisín, pronounced [ˈɔʃiːnʲ], is a prominent hero and poet in Irish mythology, particularly within the Fenian Cycle. He is the son of Fionn mac Cumhaill, leader of the Fianna, and Sadhbh, a woman transformed into a deer by a druid. Oisín's name translates to "little deer," a direct reference to his mother's form. He is celebrated for his unparalleled poetic skill and his warrior prowess, embodying the ideal of the heroic bard. His most famous tale involves his sojourn in Tír na nÓg, the land of eternal youth, with the fairy princess Niamh Chinn Óir. After what he believed to be a short time, he returned to Ireland, only to discover that centuries had passed. Upon dismounting his fairy steed, the years caught up with him, and he aged rapidly, becoming a frail old man. His stories serve as a poignant reminder of the ephemeral nature of mortal life in contrast to the timeless realm of the Otherworld.
subtitle: The Poet Warrior

keyMyths.title: Oisín in Tír na nÓg
keyMyths.description: Oisín's most famous tale recounts his journey to Tír na nÓg, the land of eternal youth, with the fairy princess Niamh Chinn Óir. He lived in this idyllic realm for what he perceived to be a few years, participating in feasts, hunts, and joyous revelry. However, a longing for his homeland eventually compelled him to return. Niamh warned him not to dismount his fairy steed on Irish soil, lest he suffer the consequences of the vastly different passage of time. His return and subsequent aging underscore the perils of crossing between the mortal and immortal realms.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Oisín
keyMyths.description: The circumstances of Oisín's birth are steeped in magic and transformation. His mother, Sadhbh, was cursed by a druid and turned into a deer. Fionn mac Cumhaill, while hunting, encountered Sadhbh in her deer form and recognized her as a woman trapped by magic. He lifted the curse, and she transformed back. They fell in love, but Sadhbh was soon lured away, leaving behind the infant Oisín. This tale emphasizes the interconnectedness of the human and animal worlds in Celtic mythology.
keyMyths.source: Rolleston, T. W. *Myths and Legends of the Celtic Race*

keyMyths.title: Oisín's Return and Lament
keyMyths.description: Upon returning to Ireland, Oisín found that his companions and the world he knew had vanished, replaced by a new age and the introduction of Christianity. While attempting to help a group of men lift a heavy stone, he fell from his horse, instantly aging to his true age of 300 years. He lamented the loss of the Fianna and the heroic age. His encounter with St. Patrick, as depicted in some versions, highlights the clash between the old pagan ways and the new Christian faith.
keyMyths.source: Ellis Davidson, H. R. *Myths and Symbols in Pagan Europe*

sources.author: Lady Gregory
sources.description: A comprehensive retelling of Irish myths and legends, including the Fenian Cycle and the tales of Oisín.
sources.text: A comprehensive retelling of Irish myths and legends, including the Fenian Cycle and the tales of Oisín.

sources.author: Rolleston, T. W.
sources.work: Myths and Legends of the Celtic Race
sources.description: A classic study of Celtic mythology, providing detailed accounts of various deities and heroes, including Oisín.
sources.text: A classic study of Celtic mythology, providing detailed accounts of various deities and heroes, including Oisín.

sources.author: Ellis Davidson, H. R.
sources.work: Myths and Symbols in Pagan Europe
sources.description: An analysis of the symbols and motifs present in pagan European cultures, including Celtic mythology, offering insights into the cultural context of Oisín's stories.
sources.text: An analysis of the symbols and motifs present in pagan European cultures, including Celtic mythology, offering insights into the cultural context of Oisín's stories.
